|  |  |  |  | threats. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
Miasma Theory (pre-1850)
Disease caused by 'bad air' (miasma). Focus on environmental
sanitation — drainage, sewage.
Germ Theory (1850–1900)
Pasteur, Koch — specific microorganisms cause specific diseases.
Focus on infection control, vaccines.
Epidemiological Triad (early
20th c.)
Host–Agent–Environment interaction. Multifactorial causation
recognized.
Social Medicine (1940s–50s)
Health determined by social and economic factors. Virchow:
'Medicine is a social science.'
Biomedical Model (1950s–70s)
Dominant curative model; organ/disease-focused; hospital-centered
care.
Comprehensive PHC (1978)
Alma Ata Declaration: 'Health for All by 2000.' Primary Health Care
as cornerstone.
Health Promotion (1986)
Ottawa Charter: Health as resource for everyday life.
Empowerment, policy, environment.
Sustainable Development
(2000s–)
MDGs → SDGs. Health as integral to development. SDG 3: Good
Health and Well-being.
Universal Health Coverage
(UHC)
Ensuring all people have access to quality health services without
financial hardship.
Planetary Health (2020s)
One Health, climate change, antimicrobial resistance as health
threats.
EXAM TIP: Conclude with: 'The concept has evolved from a negative (absence of disease) to a positive
(optimal well-being), from individual to community, from biomedical to socioecological model.' This theoretical
framing earns distinction marks.
## Q5. Discuss — 'Health is not Stethoscope and Pills'.  ★★★  (2011)
GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 10 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
This statement critiques the biomedical reductionist model of health, which equates health with clinical
diagnosis (stethoscope) and pharmacological treatment (pills).
LIMITATIONS OF THE BIOMEDICAL MODEL
• Focuses on disease rather than health promotion.
• Neglects social, psychological, environmental determinants of health.
• Hospitalo-centric and curative in orientation — addresses only 20% of health burden.
• Fails to address root causes: poverty, illiteracy, malnutrition, poor sanitation.
WHAT HEALTH TRULY REQUIRES
• Social Determinants: Income, education, employment, housing, food security.
• Primary Prevention: Immunization, sanitation, safe water, health education.
• Community Participation: Empowered communities take ownership of health.
• Intersectoral Action: Agriculture, education, infrastructure, finance — all impact health.
• Mental & Spiritual Well-being: Not addressed by any pill or stethoscope.
McKeown Thesis: The major decline in infectious disease mortality in England (18th–19th century) occurred
BEFORE the introduction of vaccines and antibiotics — attributed to better nutrition, sanitation, and living
conditions. This proves health is more than medicine.
EXAM TIP: Strong answers quote McKeown Thesis + Virchow ('Medicine is social science; politics is nothing
but medicine on a large scale') + WHO 1984 revised definition. These are gold medal differentiators.
GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 11 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only

## Grade A ★★★★★ | Asked 9x | NTRUHS SPM Paper I

SURVEILLANCE
Frequency: 9 Times Asked  |  High Priority Topic
## Q1. Define cold chain. Describe cold chain equipment and maintenance. What is AEFI?
★★★★★  (2022, 2021, 2018)
DEFINITION: COLD CHAIN
Cold chain is the system of storage and transport of vaccines at recommended temperatures (between +2°C
and +8°C for most vaccines; -15°C to -25°C for OPV) from the point of manufacture to the point of
administration, to maintain their potency and efficacy.
WHY COLD CHAIN IS CRITICAL
• Vaccines are heat-labile biological products — exposure to heat irreversibly destroys potency.
• Some vaccines (OPV) are also sensitive to freezing — freeze-sensitive vaccines include DPT, TT, Hep-B,
DT.
• Failure of cold chain is a major cause of vaccine failure and re-emergence of vaccine-preventable diseases.
COLD CHAIN EQUIPMENT AT EACH LEVEL
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Level |  |  | Equipment |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| National / State |  |  |  | Cold rooms (+2°C to +8°C) and Frozen rooms (-15°C to -25°C). |  |
|  |  |  |  | Capacity: months of supply. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Regional / District |  |  |  | Deep Freeze (-15°C to -25°C) for OPV; Ice-Lined Refrigerators |  |
|  |  |  |  | (ILR) for other vaccines. Refrigerated vans for transport. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| CHC / PHC Level |  |  |  | ILR (Ice-Lined Refrigerator) — maintains temp even during power |  |
|  |  |  |  | failure for 6–8 hrs. Deep freezer for OPV. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Sub-centre / Village |  |  |  | Vaccine Carrier (4 icepacks; maintains 4°C for 12 hrs). Cold Box |  |
|  |  |  |  | (20–22 icepacks; up to 48–72 hrs). |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Health Worker Level |  |  | Vaccine Carrier with icepacks. Hub cutter for syringe destruction. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
National / State
Cold rooms (+2°C to +8°C) and Frozen rooms (-15°C to -25°C).
Capacity: months of supply.
Regional / District
Deep Freeze (-15°C to -25°C) for OPV; Ice-Lined Refrigerators
(ILR) for other vaccines. Refrigerated vans for transport.
CHC / PHC Level
ILR (Ice-Lined Refrigerator) — maintains temp even during power
failure for 6–8 hrs. Deep freezer for OPV.
Sub-centre / Village
Vaccine Carrier (4 icepacks; maintains 4°C for 12 hrs). Cold Box
(20–22 icepacks; up to 48–72 hrs).
Health Worker Level
Vaccine Carrier with icepacks. Hub cutter for syringe destruction.
ICE-LINED REFRIGERATOR (ILR) — KEY FACTS
• Lined with ice-water mixture along the walls.
• Temperature maintained at +2°C to +8°C.
• Maintains cold for 6–8 hours after power failure — critical for power-deficit areas.
• Top-opening design prevents cold air escape when door opened.
VACCINE VIAL MONITOR (VVM)
A small adhesive label on vaccine vial with a heat-sensitive inner square:
• Stage 1: Inner square lighter than outer ring — USABLE.
• Stage 2: Inner square = outer ring colour — USABLE but use first.
• Stage 3: Inner square darker than outer ring — DO NOT USE.
• Stage 4: Inner square very dark — DISCARD immediately.
GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 12 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
COLD CHAIN MAINTENANCE
• Daily: Check temperature twice daily; record in temperature log chart; ensure proper stacking.
• Weekly: Defrost ILR when ice layer >0.5 cm (reduces efficiency).
• Monthly: Stock inventory, expiry date check, VVM inspection.
• Yearly: Annual maintenance contract (AMC) for cold chain equipment.
• General: No vaccines in door shelves; use basket/wire rack; cold box with icepacks for transport; FIFO
(First In First Out) principle.
WHAT IS AEFI?
AEFI = Adverse Event Following Immunization.
Definition: Any untoward medical occurrence which follows immunization and which does not necessarily have
a causal relationship with the usage of the vaccine. (WHO, 1999 — Glossary of Vaccine Safety Terminology.)
CLASSIFICATION OF AEFI (WHO 2012 revised)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| Vaccine product-related |  |  | Caused by intrinsic properties of vaccine. Vaccine-associated |  |
|  |  |  | paralytic polio (VAPP) from OPV. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |
| Vaccine quality defect-related |  |  | Improper manufacture — clumping, wrong concentration. Sterility |  |
|  |  |  | failure. |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  | vaccination. SIDS following vaccine. |  |

Caused by intrinsic properties of vaccine. Vaccine-associated
paralytic polio (VAPP) from OPV.
Vaccine quality defect-related
Improper manufacture — clumping, wrong concentration. Sterility
failure.
Immunization error-related
(programmatic)
Wrong dose, site, technique; contamination; reconstitution error;
cold chain failure.
Immunization anxiety-related
Vasovagal syncope, hyperventilation. Not caused by vaccine itself.
Coincidental
Temporal association only; would have occurred regardless of
vaccination. SIDS following vaccine.
COMMON AEFIs AND MANAGEMENT

redness)
Cold compress; analgesics; usually self-limiting.
Fever post-immunization
Paracetamol; monitor; usually <38.5°C and resolves in 24–48 hrs.
Anaphylaxis
Adrenaline 1:1000 IM (0.01 mg/kg); IV fluids; hospitalize.
Persistent crying (>3 hrs) —
DPT
Monitor; usually resolves; next dose use DT.

Vaccine-Associated Paralytic
Polio
Notify; shift to IPV; supportive rehabilitation.
EXAM TIP: AEFI classification (5 categories per WHO 2012) is frequently asked. Learn: Vaccine product →
Quality defect → Immunization error → Anxiety-related → Coincidental. Use this order in exams for maximum
marks.

## Q3. Surveillance of AEFI — describe in detail.  ★★★★★  (May 2025 — NEW)
OBJECTIVES OF AEFI SURVEILLANCE
• Detect and investigate AEFIs promptly to identify cause (vaccine, program, or coincidental).
• Ensure vaccine safety and maintain public confidence in immunization.
• Identify immunization errors and strengthen program quality.
• Provide data for regulatory decisions on vaccine licensing.
AEFI SURVEILLANCE SYSTEM IN INDIA
India follows the WHO-recommended AEFI Surveillance framework, operational under the Universal
Immunization Programme (UIP) and overseen by MoHFW.
Key Components:
• Case Detection: Health workers, caregivers, clinicians report AEFIs.
• Reporting: Online reporting through Co-WIN portal and AEFI reporting forms.
• Investigation: District AEFI Committee investigates serious/cluster AEFIs.
• Causality Assessment: State AEFI Committee and National AEFI Committee.
• Feedback & Action: Findings shared back to field; corrective action taken.
AEFI REPORTING CRITERIA (Serious AEFIs must be reported within 24–48 hrs)

AEFI INVESTIGATION PROCESS
1. Case Identification: History, clinical examination, onset timing after vaccination.
2. Lab Investigation: Where needed — blood culture, CSF, histopathology.
3. Causality Assessment: Using WHO 2012 classification algorithm.
4. Report Preparation: AEFI investigation form completed; batch number, storage checked.
5. Corrective Action: If immunization error found → training, supervision, cold chain review.
6. Regulatory Report: Pharmacovigilance — ADR reporting to CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control
Organisation).
CAUSALITY ASSESSMENT — WHO 2012 Algorithm
Step 1: Characterize the event (clinical diagnosis, timeline).
Step 2: Review evidence (biologic plausibility, similar cases in literature).
Step 3: Classify:

|  |  |  |  |  |  |

Evidence supports vaccine → AEFI.

NATIONAL LEVEL — AEFI COMMITTEE STRUCTURE
• Health Worker Level: Reports AEFI; provides first aid.
• District AEFI Committee: Chaired by CMHO; investigates serious AEFIs; submits report to state.
• State AEFI Committee: Expert committee; causality assessment for serious AEFIs.
• National AEFI Committee (NAEFI): Ministry level; reviews national trends; advises regulatory action.
PHARMACOVIGILANCE LINKAGE
• AEFI data feeds into Pharmacovigilance Programme of India (PvPI) under CDSCO.
• Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) Monitoring Centres across India collect vaccine safety data.
• WHO Collaborating Centre for Adverse Reaction Monitoring: Uppsala Monitoring Centre (UMC), Sweden.
EXAM TIP: NEW question (May 2025) — examiners expect: Objectives → Components of surveillance →
Reporting criteria (with timeline table) → Investigation process (numbered) → Causality assessment (WHO
2012) → Committee structure → Pharmacovigilance. This comprehensive structure earns gold medal marks.
## Q4. Describe principles of Immunization. What diseases have been eradicated and
eliminated?  ★★★★  (2021)
PRINCIPLES OF IMMUNIZATION
A. Immunological Principles:
• Primary Response: First exposure → lag phase → antibody production (IgM first, then IgG). Slow but
establishes immunological memory.
• Secondary (Anamnestic) Response: Re-exposure → rapid, large, sustained antibody production (IgG).
Basis of booster doses.
• Herd Immunity: When a critical proportion of population is immune, even non-immune individuals are
protected. Threshold varies by R0 of disease.
Herd immunity threshold = 1 − (1/R0). For measles (R0=12–18): ~92–95% coverage needed.
B. Practical Principles:
• Age: Given at optimal immunological age — not too young (maternal antibodies interfere) or too late
(disease risk window missed).
• Schedule: Multiple doses needed for some vaccines (e.g., OPV) to ensure adequate response despite
interference.
• Route and Dose: Oral (OPV), Intradermal (BCG), Intramuscular (DPT, Hep B), Subcutaneous (MR). Wrong
route → failure or adverse reaction.
• Cold Chain: Essential for heat-labile vaccines to maintain potency.
• Simultaneous Administration: Multiple vaccines at same visit do not interfere — recommended by WHO.
ERADICATED DISEASES

EXAM TIP: Distinguish: Eradication (global, permanent, no intervention needed) vs Elimination (regional,
ongoing surveillance needed) vs Control (reduction in incidence). Smallpox = only eradicated human disease.
This distinction always gets extra marks.
## Q5. Immunization in Primary Health Care.  ★★★  (2015)
PRIMARY HEALTH CARE AND IMMUNIZATION
Alma Ata Declaration (1978) identified immunization against the major infectious diseases as one of the 8
essential components of PHC. UIP (Universal Immunization Programme) is the backbone of immunization
delivery through PHC in India.
UIP — STRUCTURE AND DELIVERY
• Launched: 1985 (Expanded Programme on Immunization started 1978 by WHO).
• Coverage target: >90% for all antigens in all districts.
• Delivery points: Sub-centres, PHCs, outreach sessions, Urban Health Posts.
• Healthcare worker: ANM (Auxiliary Nurse Midwife) — primary vaccinator at village level.
KEY STRATEGIES FOR PHC-BASED IMMUNIZATION
• Routine Immunization: Fixed-site and outreach sessions at sub-centre level; ANM-led.
• Pulse Polio Immunization (PPI): Mass campaigns on National Immunization Days (NIDs) — house-to-
house follow-up.
• Mission Indradhanush: Launched 2014; targets children missed by routine immunization; >90% coverage
goal.
• Intensified Mission Indradhanush (IMI 5.0): Ongoing; focuses on zero-dose children and underserved
populations.
GOLD MEDAL MODEL ANSWERS — MD SPM (Community Medicine) | NTRUHS    Topics 8, 9 & 10 | Paper I
Page 17 of 18  |  For Academic Use Only
• Electronic Vaccine Intelligence Network (eVIN): Real-time cold chain monitoring and vaccine stock
management.
• Co-WIN Platform: Digital vaccine tracking, beneficiary management (originally for COVID-19; now
expanded).
CHALLENGES IN PHC IMMUNIZATION
• Cold chain breaks in power-deficit rural areas.
• Vaccine hesitancy — misinformation, cultural beliefs.
• Hard-to-reach populations: Tribal, migrant, urban slum dwellers.
• Shortage of ANMs; high absenteeism.
• Drop-out rates between doses (DPT1 to DPT3).
INDICATORS OF IMMUNIZATION COVERAGE

subsections.title: 11. SOCIAL / CULTURAL FACTORS IN HEALTH
subsections.content:
## Grade A ★★★★★ | Asked 9x | NTRUHS SPM Paper I

Grade A & Grade B High Priority Questions • Exam-Ready Answers
(incl. Acculturation)
Grade A | Asked 9 Times | Highest Priority
## Q1. Role of Social and Cultural Factors in Health and Disease  ★★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked in 2023, 2018, 2015, 2011 — Master this answer thoroughly.
◆ INTRODUCTION
Health is determined not only by biological factors but also by a complex interplay of social and cultural
determinants. The WHO defines health as a complete state of physical, mental, and social well-being.
Social and cultural factors constitute major determinants that profoundly influence patterns of health and
disease in communities.
◆ SOCIAL FACTORS AFFECTING HEALTH
1. Socioeconomic Status (SES)
SES is one of the strongest determinants of health. It encompasses income, education, and occupation.
Low SES is associated with:
● Higher prevalence of infectious diseases (TB, malaria, cholera)
● Poor nutrition and higher rates of malnutrition
● Limited access to healthcare services
● Increased exposure to environmental hazards
● Higher infant and maternal mortality rates
2. Education
Education directly influences health literacy, health-seeking behavior, and disease prevention:
● Educated mothers have lower rates of infant mortality
● Higher education correlates with better hygiene practices
● Education enables understanding of health information and compliance with treatment
3. Occupation and Working Conditions
Occupational factors influence both physical and mental health:
● Hazardous occupations: Silicosis (miners), Byssinosis (cotton workers), Mesothelioma
(asbestos workers)
● Psychosocial stress in high-pressure occupations leads to hypertension and cardiovascular
diseases
● Agricultural workers exposed to pesticides face neurotoxicity risks
4. Housing and Living Conditions
● Overcrowding facilitates transmission of respiratory diseases (TB, influenza)
● Poor sanitation increases diarrheal diseases
● Indoor air pollution from biomass fuels causes COPD and respiratory infections
● Lack of safe drinking water leads to waterborne diseases
5. Social Support and Networks
● Strong social networks act as protective factors against mental illness
● Social isolation increases risk of depression, cardiovascular disease, and premature death
● Community cohesion improves disaster preparedness and health outcomes
6. Gender and Inequality
● Women face gender-based discrimination affecting access to healthcare
● Gender inequality leads to nutritional neglect of girl children
● Female genital mutilation (FGM) practiced in certain societies causes severe health
complications
◆ CULTURAL FACTORS AFFECTING HEALTH
1. Food Habits and Dietary Practices
Cultural beliefs profoundly influence dietary patterns:
● Vegetarianism (practiced in many Hindu communities): May lead to iron, B12, zinc deficiency
● Halal and Kosher dietary restrictions may limit access to certain nutrients
● Festivals with feasting: Contribute to obesity and metabolic syndrome
● Taboo foods during pregnancy (e.g., avoiding eggs, meat in some tribes): Can cause maternal
malnutrition
● Colostrum discarding in many rural communities denies newborns passive immunity
2. Religious Beliefs and Practices
● Blood transfusion refusal (Jehovah's Witnesses): Can be life-threatening
● Faith healing over modern medicine: Delays treatment of serious conditions
● Pilgrimage gatherings (Kumbh Mela, Hajj): Can facilitate mass disease outbreaks (cholera,
meningitis)
● Cow urine consumption and other religious remedies may cause harm
● Fasting during Ramadan / Navratri: Can affect diabetic control
3. Traditional Medicine and Folk Beliefs
● Preference for traditional healers (Vaids, Hakims) over allopathic doctors delays definitive
treatment
● Use of certain herbs (e.g., Aristolochia): Can cause nephropathy
● Heavy metal-containing Ayurvedic preparations: Risk of lead/mercury toxicity
4. Marriage Practices
● Consanguineous marriages (cousin marriages): Increase risk of autosomal recessive genetic
disorders (thalassemia, sickle cell anemia)
● Child marriage: Associated with early pregnancy, maternal and neonatal complications
● Polygamy: Increases risk of STIs including HIV/AIDS
5. Hygiene and Sanitation Practices
● Open defecation practices (prevalent in rural India): Cause fecal-oral transmission of diseases
● Use of shared utensils: Spreads hepatitis A, typhoid
● Non-use of latrines due to cultural habits: Perpetuates soil-transmitted helminthiasis
6. Birth Practices
● Home deliveries by untrained dais: Lead to neonatal tetanus, birth injuries
● Applying cow dung on umbilical stump: Causes neonatal tetanus
● Early introduction of solids: Can cause diarrhea and malnutrition in infants
7. Death and Burial Practices
● Open-air cremation near water sources: Can contaminate drinking water
● Communal burial practices during epidemics: Can spread diseases if infected bodies are not
handled properly
◆ MECHANISMS BY WHICH SOCIAL/CULTURAL FACTORS INFLUENCE HEALTH

◆ CONTROL MEASURES
● Health education targeting cultural myths and misconceptions
● Involvement of community leaders, religious heads, and traditional healers in health programs
● Legislation against harmful practices (child marriage, FGM)
● Culturally sensitive healthcare delivery — employing local health workers
● Social mobilization campaigns (e.g., Pulse Polio, Swachh Bharat Abhiyan)
● Multi-sectoral approach: Linking health with education, employment, and social welfare
★ EXAM TIP: Conclude with: 'Social and cultural factors are key determinants of health. Any successful
public health intervention must be culturally appropriate and community-centered.'
## Q2. Culture, Social Structure & Community Health  ★★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Dec 2024 NEW — Focus on definitions + structure with examples.
◆ DEFINITION OF KEY TERMS
Culture: The integrated pattern of human knowledge, beliefs, behaviors, arts, morals, laws, customs,
and habits acquired and transmitted by members of a society. (E.B. Tylor, 1871)
Social Structure: The organized pattern of social relationships and social institutions that together
compose society — including family, caste, religion, class, and gender.
Community: A group of people living in the same locality with shared interests, values, and
governance.
◆ COMPONENTS OF SOCIAL STRUCTURE RELEVANT TO HEALTH
A. Family Structure
● Nuclear family: More privacy, better child spacing; but less support during illness
● Joint/extended family: Better support system but overcrowding risk; more disease transmission
● Matriarchal vs. Patriarchal families: Affect decision-making for healthcare
B. Caste System
● Lower castes historically denied access to health resources
● Caste-based discrimination affects mental health of marginalized groups
● Scheduled caste communities have higher rates of malnutrition and poverty
C. Religion and Community
● Religious affiliation affects dietary practices, birth rates, and disease patterns
● Community identity influences vaccine acceptance and health-seeking behavior
D. Social Class
● Lower social class = higher disease burden (inverse care law — Julian Tudor Hart)
● Social class determines living conditions, nutrition, and access to medical care
◆ HOW SOCIAL STRUCTURE AFFECTS HEALTH — KEY PATHWAYS

◆ CULTURE AND ITS INFLUENCE ON HEALTH
● Cultural norms define what is considered 'healthy' or 'sick' — affecting illness behavior
● Culture determines healthcare-seeking patterns (traditional vs. modern medicine)
● Cultural practices can promote health (yoga, meditation, balanced diets) or harm health
(tobacco chewing, purdah system reducing female mobility)
● Stigma around mental illness, HIV/AIDS — deeply rooted in cultural beliefs — hinders treatment
◆ IMPLICATIONS FOR PUBLIC HEALTH
● Health programs must be designed with cultural sensitivity
● Community participation is essential for sustained behavioral change
● Social structure should be mapped during needs assessment of communities
● Laws must address structural inequities (Reservation system, PESA Act for tribals)
★ EXAM TIP: This question tests your ability to link sociology with public health. Use the Lalonde Model of
Health (biology, environment, lifestyle, healthcare organization) and mention social determinants of health
(SDH) as per WHO Commission, 2008.
## Q3. Socio-Cultural Factors in Pregnant and Lactating Women (India)
★★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: May 2024 NEW — Link cultural practices to maternal and neonatal outcomes.
◆ INTRODUCTION
Pregnant and lactating women in India are significantly influenced by socio-cultural practices. These
factors can either protect or endanger maternal and infant health, and must be understood for effective
MCH (Maternal and Child Health) programming.
◆ HARMFUL SOCIO-CULTURAL PRACTICES IN PREGNANCY
A. Dietary Taboos during Pregnancy
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

|  | sources) |  |  | low birth weight |  |  |  |  |

|  | in pregnancy |  |  | growth restriction |  |  |  |  |

sources)

low birth weight

B. Harmful Traditional Practices
● Delivery by traditional birth attendants (Dais) without sterile technique: Cause of neonatal
tetanus (applying cow dung on cord)
● Restriction of colostrum: Deprives newborn of passive immunity, secretory IgA, and first nutrition
● Early initiation of prelacteal feeds (honey, ghutti, cow's milk): Cause diarrhea, infection
● Postpartum isolation ('Chilla' period): Prevents newborn exposure but also restricts maternal
nutrition
● Female seclusion ('Purdah'): Reduces access to antenatal care services
● Son preference: Leads to neglect of nutritional needs of pregnant girls
C. Practices Harmful during Lactation
● Feeding cow's milk/formula early: Breast milk rejection and risk of infection
● Maternal dietary restrictions during breastfeeding: Reduces quality and quantity of breast milk
● Early weaning due to social pressure: Deprives infant of breastfeeding benefits
◆ BENEFICIAL SOCIO-CULTURAL PRACTICES
● Valsa system of food supplementation by community women
● Extended joint family providing rest and support to new mothers
● Traditional foods rich in iron: Jaggery, leafy vegetables — though often avoided due to myths
● Prolonged breastfeeding culturally encouraged in some communities
◆ PUBLIC HEALTH STRATEGIES
● POSHAN Abhiyaan (National Nutrition Mission): Targets malnutrition in women and children
● Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY): Promotes institutional deliveries
● ASHA workers: Act as cultural bridges, counseling women on harmful practices
● Behavior Change Communication (BCC): Targeted campaigns addressing myths
● Kishori Shakti Yojana: Nutritional and reproductive health education for adolescent girls
★ EXAM TIP: Mention POSHAN Abhiyaan, JSY, Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana (PMMVY) for
brownie points. The examiner expects you to link cultural practices to specific maternal/neonatal health
indicators.
## Q4. Social and Cultural Factors: Women and Children  ★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked 2023 — Focus on gender-specific factors + child health.
◆ WOMEN: HEALTH-INFLUENCING SOCIO-CULTURAL FACTORS
A. Gender Discrimination
● Female foeticide and infanticide: Declining sex ratio (Sex Ratio at Birth: 930 per 1000 males in
India — Census 2011)
● Girl child nutrition deprivation: Higher rates of stunting and wasting in females
● Early marriage and early pregnancy: Leading cause of maternal mortality; <18 years marriage
increases obstetric risk
● Limited reproductive autonomy: Low contraceptive use, high unmet need
B. Cultural Factors Affecting Women
● Purdah system: Restricts mobility, limits access to healthcare
● Dowry system: Causes domestic violence, affecting mental and physical health
● Widow restrictions: Nutritional and psychosocial deprivation
● Preference for male children: Leads to sex-selective abortion (PC-PNDT Act, 1994 to combat
this)
◆ CHILDREN: HEALTH-INFLUENCING SOCIO-CULTURAL FACTORS
A. Nutritional Factors
● Colostrum discarding: Increases neonatal mortality
● Late breastfeeding initiation: WHO recommends within 1 hour of birth
● Inappropriate complementary feeding: Diarrhea, growth failure
● Use of feeding bottles: Increases infection risk (bottle-mouth syndrome)
B. Traditional Practices Affecting Children
● Tying of fontanelle with cloth (to 'close the soft spot'): No benefit, can cause head injury
● Use of kajal (Kohl) in eyes: Risk of lead poisoning
● Ghutti (honey/herbal preparation): Botulism risk in neonates
● Delayed vaccination due to cultural beliefs: Increases susceptibility to VPDs
C. Social Factors
● Poverty: Main driver of child malnutrition and mortality
● Mother's education: Most powerful predictor of child health outcomes
● Birth order: Higher-order births have worse nutrition and healthcare
● Spacing of children: <2 years leads to 'sibling competition' for nutrition
◆ GOVERNMENT INITIATIVES
● PC-PNDT Act (1994): Prevention of sex-selective abortion
● Beti Bachao, Beti Padhao: Addressing girl child neglect
● ICDS (Integrated Child Development Services): Nutrition, immunization, preschool education
● IMNCI (Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness)
● Mid-Day Meal Scheme: Nutrition and school retention
★ EXAM TIP: This answer combines both women AND children. Use a tabular comparison if time permits.
Mention specific government programs — they attract marks.
## Q5. Social and Behavioral Determinants of Health and Disease  ★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked 2014 — Theoretical question; include Lalonde model.
◆ DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH: DEFINITION
Determinants of health are the range of personal, social, economic, and environmental factors that
influence health status. The Lalonde Report (1974) identified four broad health field concepts:
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Determinant Category |  |  | Examples |  |  | % Contribution (approx.) |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Human Biology |  |  | Genetics, age, sex, immunity |  |  | ~20% |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
Human Biology

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

economic

alcohol

Healthcare Organization

◆ KEY SOCIAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH (WHO Framework)
● Income and social protection
● Education and literacy
● Unemployment and job insecurity
● Working life conditions
● Food insecurity
● Housing and basic amenities
● Early childhood development
● Social inclusion and non-discrimination
● Structural conflict (war, displacement)
● Access to affordable health services
◆ BEHAVIORAL DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH
A. Tobacco Use
● Smoking: Lung cancer, COPD, cardiovascular disease, peptic ulcer
● Smokeless tobacco: Oral cancers, hypertension
● Cultural factors: Betel nut chewing, hookah smoking in certain communities
B. Alcohol Consumption
● Liver cirrhosis, cardiomyopathy, pancreatitis
● Social consequences: Domestic violence, road traffic accidents, poverty
● Fetal Alcohol Syndrome: Alcohol during pregnancy
C. Dietary Behavior
● High fat, sugar, sodium intake: Obesity, T2DM, hypertension, CVD
● Low fiber diet: Colorectal cancer, constipation
● Malnutrition behaviors: Crash dieting, anorexia, food insecurity
D. Physical Inactivity
● Sedentary lifestyle: Leads to obesity, osteoporosis, metabolic syndrome
● Screen time increases: Associated with childhood obesity epidemic
E. Sexual Behavior
● Unprotected sex: HIV/AIDS, STIs, unintended pregnancies
● Multiple partnerships: Increases STI transmission network
F. Substance Abuse
● Injection drug use: HIV, Hepatitis B and C transmission
● Drug abuse: Mental illness, social disruption, crime
◆ CONTROL STRATEGIES
● Behavior Change Communication (BCC) — SBCC approaches
● Health promotion — Ottawa Charter (1986): Healthy public policy, supportive environments,
community action
● Risk factor reduction: NPCDCS, National Tobacco Control Program
● Legislative measures: COTPA (2003), Food Safety and Standards Act
★ EXAM TIP: Always mention the Ottawa Charter for Health Promotion (1986) when discussing behavioral
determinants. The 5 action areas are: Build healthy public policy, Create supportive environments,
Strengthen community action, Develop personal skills, Reorient health services.
## Q6. Acculturation — Definition and Health Implications  ★★★
★ EXAM TIP: May 2024 NEW — Define clearly + give the Berry Model + health effects.
◆ DEFINITION
Acculturation is the process of cultural and psychological change that occurs when two cultures come
into sustained contact. It involves changes in values, behaviors, attitudes, and social structures of one or
both cultures.
(Redfield, Linton & Herskovits, 1936): 'Acculturation comprehends those phenomena which result when
groups of individuals having different cultures come into continuous first-hand contact, with subsequent
changes in the original culture patterns of either or both groups.'
◆ BERRY'S MODEL OF ACCULTURATION (4 Strategies)
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Strategy |  |  |  | Maintain Heritage |  |  | Adopt Host |  | Health Outcome |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | Culture? |  |  | Culture? |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Integration |  |  | Yes |  |  | Yes |  |  | Best mental health outcomes |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Assimilation |  |  | No |  |  | Yes |  |  | Moderate stress |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Separation |  |  | Yes |  |  | No |  |  | Cultural isolation, higher stress |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Marginalization |  |  | No |  |  | No |  |  |  | Worst health outcomes — |  |

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

'Acculturative Stress'
◆ ACCULTURATIVE STRESS
When individuals cannot adapt to a new culture, they experience 'acculturative stress' (Culture Shock),
characterized by:
● Anxiety, depression, confusion, identity crisis
● Psychosomatic complaints: Headache, insomnia, fatigue
● Increased risk of substance abuse and psychiatric disorders
● Alienation from both heritage and host cultures
◆ HEALTH IMPLICATIONS OF ACCULTURATION
A. Nutritional Changes
● Shift from traditional to Western diet: High fat, high sugar, processed foods
● Increase in obesity, T2DM, cardiovascular disease among immigrants
● Loss of traditional protective dietary practices (e.g., Mediterranean diet)
B. Behavioral Changes
● Adoption of risky behaviors: Alcohol, tobacco, drugs — common in acculturating youth
● Change in sexual behavior norms: Increased STI risk
● Physical inactivity: Sedentary Western lifestyle replaces active traditional living
C. Mental Health Impact
● 'Immigrant Paradox': Recent immigrants often have BETTER mental health than native-born or
long-settled immigrants (due to social cohesion of original community)
● Second-generation immigrants: Higher rates of depression, identity conflicts
● Acculturation-related domestic violence: Cultural conflicts in family settings
D. Healthcare-Seeking Behavior
● Language barriers reduce access to healthcare
● Distrust of host country medical system
● Cultural incompetence of healthcare providers: Poor patient-provider communication
E. Positive Effects of Acculturation
● Adoption of healthy lifestyle habits from host culture
● Improved sanitation and hygiene practices
● Increased vaccination rates and preventive care utilization
● Access to modern medical treatments
◆ ACCULTURATING GROUPS IN INDIA
● Tribal populations integrating into mainstream society (e.g., Adivasis in urban areas)
● Rural-to-urban migrants: Face dual burden of traditional diseases + urban NCDs
● Diaspora communities abroad
● Refugee populations (e.g., Rohingya, Sri Lankan Tamils)
◆ HEALTH INTERVENTIONS
● Culturally sensitive health services — 'Cultural Competency' training for health workers
● Community health workers from same ethnic backgrounds
● Bilingual health education materials
● Mental health support for acculturating populations
● WHO Social Determinants framework to address structural barriers
★ EXAM TIP: Memorize Berry's 4 acculturation strategies — they appear as 1-mark questions. Integration =
best health. Marginalization = worst health.

subsections.title: 12. SAMPLING METHODS / PROBABILITY SAMPLING
subsections.content:
## Grade B ★★★★ | Asked 7x | NTRUHS SPM Paper I

SAMPLING TECHNIQUES
Grade B | Asked 7 Times | High Priority
## Q1. What is Sampling? Methods of Sampling with Examples  ★★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked 2021, 2018 — The master answer covering all sampling types.
◆ DEFINITION OF SAMPLING
Sampling: The process of selecting a representative subset (sample) from a larger group
(population/universe) for the purpose of a study, such that inferences drawn from the sample can be
generalized to the population.
Universe / Population: The entire group of individuals or items that meet the criteria for a study
Sample: A subset of the population selected for study
Sampling Frame: A list of all elements in the population from which the sample is drawn (e.g., voter
list, school register)
Sampling Unit: The unit selected at each stage of sampling (individual, household, village)
◆ WHY SAMPLING?
● Cost-effective: Studying entire population is expensive
● Time-saving: Faster data collection
● Feasibility: Some populations are infinite or inaccessible
● Greater accuracy: Intensive study of fewer subjects yields better quality data
● Destructive testing: Some tests destroy the subject (food quality testing, drug testing)
◆ CLASSIFICATION OF SAMPLING METHODS
SAMPLING METHODS
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Probability Sampling |  |  | Non-Probability Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Simple Random Sampling (SRS) |  |  | Convenience Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Systematic Random Sampling |  |  | Purposive / Judgmental Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Stratified Random Sampling |  |  | Quota Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Cluster Sampling |  |  | Snowball Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Multi-stage Sampling |  |  | Accidental Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
Simple Random Sampling (SRS)
Convenience Sampling
Systematic Random Sampling
Purposive / Judgmental Sampling
Stratified Random Sampling
Quota Sampling
Cluster Sampling
Snowball Sampling
Multi-stage Sampling
Accidental Sampling
◆ A. PROBABILITY SAMPLING METHODS
In probability sampling, every element in the population has a known, non-zero probability of being
selected. It allows for statistical inference and generalization.
1. Simple Random Sampling (SRS)
Each unit in the population has an equal and independent chance of selection.
● Methods: Lottery method, Random number tables, Computer-generated random numbers
● Example: Selecting 50 students from 500 by assigning numbers and using a random number
table
Advantages: Unbiased, simple, gold standard
Disadvantages: Requires complete sampling frame; impractical for large, geographically dispersed
populations
2. Systematic Random Sampling
Elements are selected at regular intervals from the sampling frame after a random start.
● Sampling interval (k) = Population Size (N) / Sample Size (n)
● Example: Selecting every 10th patient from an OPD register after randomly choosing a starting
point between 1-10
Advantage: Simple, no complete list needed, spread across population
Disadvantage: Periodicity bias — if there is a periodic pattern in the list
3. Stratified Random Sampling
Population is divided into homogeneous subgroups (strata) based on a relevant characteristic, then SRS
is applied within each stratum.
● Proportionate stratification: Same proportion sampled from each stratum
● Disproportionate stratification: Higher sampling fraction from smaller/important strata
● Example: Stratifying by gender, age group, socioeconomic class, then sampling within each
group
Advantage: Ensures representation of all subgroups; reduces sampling error
Disadvantage: Requires knowledge of stratification variable for all population members
4. Cluster Sampling
Population is divided into natural groups (clusters — e.g., villages, schools, blocks). Clusters are
randomly selected and ALL individuals within selected clusters are studied.
● Single-stage cluster: All units within selected cluster are studied
● Two-stage cluster: Random sample within selected cluster
● Example: Selecting 10 villages randomly from 100 villages and studying all households in
selected villages
● USED IN: EPI coverage surveys (WHO 30x7 cluster sampling method)
Advantage: Cost-effective, no need for complete sampling frame of individuals
Disadvantage: Cluster members tend to be similar (intracluster correlation) — less precise; requires
larger sample size
5. Multi-stage Sampling
Sampling is done in multiple stages with different units at each stage.
● Stage 1: Districts → Stage 2: Blocks → Stage 3: Villages → Stage 4: Households → Stage 5:
Individuals
● Used in NFHS (National Family Health Survey), NSSO surveys
Advantage: Practical for large-scale national surveys; cost-effective
Disadvantage: Complex design; errors may accumulate at each stage; requires expertise
◆ B. NON-PROBABILITY SAMPLING METHODS
Selection is based on non-random criteria. Not all elements have a known probability of selection. Cannot
generalize to the population.
1. Convenience Sampling
● Selecting subjects based on availability and willingness
● Example: Surveying patients in OPD, passersby on street
● Use: Pilot studies, exploratory research
2. Purposive (Judgmental) Sampling
● Researcher selects subjects based on their own judgment about who will be most informative
● Example: Selecting expert health administrators for a policy study
● Use: Qualitative research, key informant interviews
3. Quota Sampling
● Population divided into subgroups; interviewer fills predetermined quotas
● Example: 'Interview 20 males and 20 females between ages 18-65 from this area'
● Used in market research, opinion polls
4. Snowball Sampling
● Existing subjects recruit future subjects from their network
● Example: Studying IV drug users — hard-to-reach population; one user refers others
● Used for hidden/stigmatized populations: IV drug users, sex workers, LGBTQ+ communities
◆ COMPARISON: PROBABILITY vs. NON-PROBABILITY SAMPLING
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Feature |  |  | Probability Sampling |  |  | Non-Probability Sampling |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Selection basis |  |  | Random (chance) |  |  | Non-random (judgment) |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

★ EXAM TIP: In exam, always define sampling → classify → explain each type → give example →
advantages/disadvantages. End with a comparison table. This ensures full marks.
## Q2. Various Methods of Sampling in Medical Research  ★★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked 2023 — Same as Q1 but emphasize medical research applications.
◆ SAMPLING IN MEDICAL RESEARCH: CONTEXT
Medical research involves studying populations to understand disease patterns, evaluate interventions,
or assess healthcare needs. Appropriate sampling ensures validity, reliability, and generalizability of
research findings.
◆ SAMPLING METHODS IN MEDICAL RESEARCH CONTEXT
Clinical Trials
● Convenience or purposive sampling for Phase I (safety) trials
● Stratified randomization to ensure balanced treatment groups
● Block randomization: Ensures equal allocation in subgroups
Epidemiological Surveys
● Simple random or systematic sampling for disease prevalence studies
● Multi-stage cluster sampling for national surveys (NFHS, DLHS)
● Stratified sampling by age, sex, region for representativeness
Case-Control Studies
● Cases: All incident cases from a defined population (census of cases)
● Controls: Random sample from the source population (incidence density sampling)
Cohort Studies
● Defined cohort selected from a population (occupational cohort, birth cohort)
● Follow-up of entire cohort — no sampling within; but sampling of the initial cohort
Health Service Evaluation
● Cluster sampling of health facilities
● Systematic sampling of patient records
◆ SAMPLE SIZE DETERMINATION
Before sampling, adequate sample size must be calculated using:
● Prevalence of condition in population (P)
● Desired precision (d) — allowable error
● Confidence level (Z — usually 1.96 for 95% CI)
● Formula (for proportions): n = Z² × P × (1-P) / d²
● Add 10-20% for non-response / attrition
★ EXAM TIP: In medical research, mention: Sampling bias (selection bias), response bias, non-response
bias. Examiner expects awareness of bias in context of each method.
## Q3. Probability Sampling Techniques in Research Study  ★★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Dec 2024 NEW — Focus purely on PROBABILITY sampling — all 5 types in detail.
◆ DEFINITION
Probability sampling is a sampling technique where every unit of the population has a known, non-zero
probability of being selected. It is the foundation of scientific, unbiased epidemiological research.
◆ CHARACTERISTICS OF PROBABILITY SAMPLING
● Randomness: Selection based on chance, not investigator's choice
● Representativeness: Sample mirrors the population
● Quantifiable error: Sampling error can be estimated
● Inferential: Allows generalization from sample to population
● Unbiased: Eliminates selection bias
◆ THE FIVE PROBABILITY SAMPLING TECHNIQUES
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Method |  |  | Key Feature |  |  | Best Used For |  |  | Example in Health Research |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

◆ WHO EPI CLUSTER SAMPLING (30×7 Method)
The WHO Expanded Programme on Immunization (EPI) uses a specific cluster sampling methodology
for vaccine coverage surveys:
● Step 1: Obtain population list of all villages/areas in the region
● Step 2: Calculate cumulative population and sampling interval
● Step 3: Use PPS (Probability Proportionate to Size) to select 30 clusters
● Step 4: In each cluster, interview 7 children aged 12-23 months
● Total sample = 30 × 7 = 210 children
● Household selection: 'Modified EPI method' — random direction spin from center of village
◆ SOURCES OF SAMPLING ERROR
● Random sampling error: Due to chance (reduced by increasing sample size)
● Systematic error / bias: Due to faulty sampling design (cannot be corrected after sampling)
● Non-response bias: When non-respondents differ from respondents
● Frame error: Sampling frame does not match target population
★ EXAM TIP: For probability sampling, learn the formulas: SRS = equal probability; Systematic k=N/n; For
Stratified: Proportionate allocation ni = n × Ni/N. These formulae show depth of knowledge.
## Q4. Define Sampling. Write about Cluster Sampling in Detail  ★★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked 2018 — Half the answer is on cluster sampling specifically.
◆ DEFINITION OF SAMPLING
Sampling is the process of selecting a representative subset from a defined population, with the intention
that conclusions drawn from the sample will be applicable to the population as a whole.
◆ CLUSTER SAMPLING — DETAILED DESCRIPTION
Definition
Cluster sampling is a probability sampling technique in which the population is divided into groups
(clusters), clusters are randomly selected, and all units within selected clusters are studied (single-stage)
or a random sample is drawn from within selected clusters (two-stage).
When to Use Cluster Sampling
● Population is geographically spread and a complete sampling frame of individuals is unavailable
● Cost-effective study of widely distributed populations
● When clusters (natural groups) exist in the population
Types of Cluster Sampling
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Type |  |  | Description |  |  | Example |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Single-stage cluster |  |  |  | All units in selected clusters |  |  | All households in 5 randomly selected |  |
|  |  |  |  | studied |  |  | villages |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Two-stage cluster |  |  |  | Sample drawn within selected |  |  | 30 random households from each of 10 |  |
|  |  |  |  | clusters |  |  | selected villages |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Multi-stage cluster |  |  |  | Multiple levels of sampling |  | NFHS survey design |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | within clusters |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| PPS Cluster |  |  |  | Clusters selected with |  | WHO EPI 30×7 method |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | probability proportional to size |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
Single-stage cluster
All units in selected clusters
studied
All households in 5 randomly selected
villages
Two-stage cluster
Sample drawn within selected
clusters
30 random households from each of 10
selected villages
Multi-stage cluster
Multiple levels of sampling
within clusters
NFHS survey design
PPS Cluster
Clusters selected with
probability proportional to size
WHO EPI 30×7 method
Steps in Cluster Sampling
● Step 1: Define the population and identify natural clusters (e.g., villages, school classes,
hospital wards)
● Step 2: List all clusters with their sizes
● Step 3: Calculate sampling interval if using PPS
● Step 4: Randomly select required number of clusters
● Step 5: Study all (or a sample of) units in each selected cluster
Advantages of Cluster Sampling
● No need for a complete individual-level sampling frame — only cluster-level list required
● Cost-effective — resources concentrated in selected clusters
● Logistically feasible for large, geographically dispersed populations
● Suitable for EPI surveys, community health surveys
Disadvantages of Cluster Sampling
● Loss of statistical efficiency — members of same cluster tend to be similar (intracluster
correlation = ICC)
● Design Effect (DEFF): Must multiply sample size by design effect (usually 1.5-2.0 for cluster
sampling)
● More complex analysis required
● Clusters may not be homogeneous — some very large, some very small (PPS addresses this)
Design Effect (DEFF)
DEFF = Variance of cluster sample / Variance of SRS
● DEFF > 1 means cluster sampling is less efficient than SRS
● To achieve same precision: Sample size from cluster design = SRS sample size × DEFF
★ EXAM TIP: Cluster sampling is tested repeatedly. Always mention: ICC (Intracluster Correlation
Coefficient), Design Effect (DEFF), PPS selection, and WHO EPI 30×7 method.
## Q5. Sampling Methods for Community-Based Research  ★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Nov 2023 NEW — Focus on practical community health research applications.
◆ COMMUNITY-BASED RESEARCH: OVERVIEW
Community-based research aims to study health problems in natural community settings. It includes
prevalence surveys, intervention studies, surveillance, and health needs assessments. Sampling in
community research must balance scientific rigor with practical constraints.
◆ MOST COMMONLY USED METHODS IN COMMUNITY RESEARCH
1. Multi-stage Stratified Cluster Sampling
Most widely used in large community surveys (NFHS, DLHS, AHS, NNMB):
● Stage 1: Select districts (strata = rural/urban)
● Stage 2: Select villages/wards using PPS
● Stage 3: Select households using systematic sampling
● Stage 4: Select individuals within households
2. Simple Random Sampling
● Used when community is small and a complete sampling frame exists (e.g., school children,
registered patients)
3. Systematic Sampling
● Selecting every kth household along a street / from a PHC register
4. Stratified Sampling
● Stratify by rural/urban, gender, age group, socioeconomic class
● Ensures proportionate representation of all groups
5. Community-Based Participatory Sampling
● Community members involved in sampling design
● Uses local knowledge to identify hard-to-reach groups
◆ PRACTICAL CHALLENGES IN COMMUNITY SAMPLING
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Challenge |  |  | Solution |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | No updated census/list |  |  | Use satellite maps, GPS enumeration |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | High non-response rate |  |  | Multiple follow-up visits; incentives |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Seasonal migration |  |  | Stratify by season; note seasonal population |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Language barriers |  |  | Local language questionnaires, local surveyors |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Hard-to-reach populations |  |  | Snowball or RDS (Respondent-Driven Sampling) |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
No updated census/list
Use satellite maps, GPS enumeration
High non-response rate
Multiple follow-up visits; incentives
Seasonal migration
Stratify by season; note seasonal population
Language barriers
Local language questionnaires, local surveyors
Hard-to-reach populations
Snowball or RDS (Respondent-Driven Sampling)
◆ QUALITY CONTROL IN COMMUNITY SAMPLING
● Pilot study before main survey (5-10% of sample size)
● Training and standardization of field investigators
● Supervision and back-checks of completed forms
● Data entry double-checking
● Consistency checks during analysis
★ EXAM TIP: Community-based research also uses Rapid Assessment Procedures (RAP) and Lot Quality
Assurance Sampling (LQAS) — mention these for extra marks.
## Q6. Define Randomization and Explain Methods of Randomization
★★★
★ EXAM TIP: Asked 2023 — Related to sampling but focuses on experimental design.
◆ DEFINITION OF RANDOMIZATION
Randomization is the process of randomly assigning subjects to different treatment or comparison groups
in an experimental study (especially Randomized Controlled Trials — RCTs), such that each participant
has an equal and independent chance of being assigned to any group.
Randomization is the hallmark of experimental study design and the most effective method of controlling
confounding variables.
◆ PURPOSE OF RANDOMIZATION
● Eliminates selection bias in group allocation
● Ensures comparability of groups at baseline (known and unknown confounders balanced)
● Allows valid statistical inference
● Strengthens causality of observed associations
● Foundation of Evidence-Based Medicine (EBM)
◆ METHODS OF RANDOMIZATION
1. Simple Randomization
Each participant is independently assigned to a group based on a random process.
● Method: Coin toss (Heads = Treatment, Tails = Control)
● Random number table: Even = Group A, Odd = Group B
● Computer random number generator
Advantage: Simple, easy to implement
Disadvantage: Small samples may yield unequal group sizes by chance
2. Block Randomization (Permuted Block Randomization)
Participants are randomized in blocks to ensure equal group sizes at any point in the trial.
● Block size (b): Usually 4, 6, or 8
● Example (block size 4): All permutations of AABB = AABB, ABAB, ABBA, BABA, BAAB, BBAA
● Randomly select one permutation per block; ensures groups balanced every b patients
Advantage: Ensures equal group sizes; important if recruitment may stop early
Disadvantage: Blinding can be compromised if block size is known
3. Stratified Randomization
Randomization is performed within strata defined by important prognostic factors.
● Strata defined by: Age, sex, disease severity, center in multi-center trials
● Block randomization applied within each stratum
● Example: Stratify hypertensive patients by severity (mild/moderate/severe), then randomize
within each group
Advantage: Ensures balance across important prognostic variables; especially important in small trials
Disadvantage: Complex with many strata; limited to 2-3 stratifying variables
4. Minimization
An adaptive randomization method that minimizes imbalance across multiple prognostic factors
simultaneously.
● Each new participant assigned to the group that minimizes overall imbalance
● More efficient than stratified randomization for multiple factors
● Used in large multi-center clinical trials
5. Cluster Randomization
Groups (clusters) rather than individuals are randomized.
● Used in community intervention trials (e.g., health education program)
● Example: 10 villages randomized to receive intervention or control
● Advantage: Prevents contamination between groups
● Disadvantage: Loss of efficiency; needs design effect correction; ICC must be accounted for
6. Adaptive Randomization
Randomization probabilities change based on accumulating trial data.
● Response-adaptive: More patients allocated to better-performing treatment
● Covariate-adaptive: Minimizes covariate imbalance
● Ethical advantage: Fewer patients receive inferior treatment
◆ ALLOCATION CONCEALMENT vs. BLINDING
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Concept |  |  | Definition |  |  | Purpose |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Allocation Concealment |  |  |  | Hiding treatment allocation |  | Prevents selection bias BEFORE allocation |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | sequence from those enrolling |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | patients |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Blinding |  |  |  | Hiding treatment allocation from |  | Prevents performance and detection bias |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | participants/investigators AFTER |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | allocation |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
Allocation Concealment
Hiding treatment allocation
sequence from those enrolling
patients
Prevents selection bias BEFORE
allocation
Blinding
Hiding treatment allocation from
participants/investigators AFTER
allocation
Prevents performance and
detection bias
◆ SEQUENCE GENERATION vs. ALLOCATION CONCEALMENT
● Sequence generation: Creating the randomization sequence (computer, tables)
● Allocation concealment: Implementing the sequence without bias (sealed envelopes, SNOSE —
Sequentially Numbered Opaque Sealed Envelopes, central telephone randomization)
★ EXAM TIP: CONSORT 2010 Statement requires reporting of: randomization sequence generation method,
allocation concealment mechanism, and implementation details. Mention CONSORT in exam answers for
extra marks.
QUICK REVISION SUMMARY
Topic 11: Social/Cultural Factors — Key Points

subsections.title: 13. SURVEILLANCE — Types, NCD & AEFI
subsections.content:
## Grade B ★★★★ | Asked 6x | NTRUHS SPM Paper I

Surveillance
## Q1. Types of Surveillance with Examples  ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Oct 2025 — NEW)
Definition
Surveillance is the continuous, systematic collection, analysis, interpretation, and dissemination of
health data for planning, implementation, and evaluation of public health programmes. (WHO)
★ Mnemonic: "C-A-I-D" — Collect, Analyse, Interpret, Disseminate
A. Based on Source of Data
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Type |  |  | Description |  |  | Example |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Passive |  |  | Routine data from health facilities; least effort |  |  |  | OPD registers, death |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  | certificates |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Active |  |  | Active search by investigators; more accurate |  |  |  | House-to-house polio |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  | surveys |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Sentinel |  |  |  | Selected sentinel sites represent whole |  | Sentinel STI/HIV clinics |  |  |
|  |  |  |  | population |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Syndromic |  |  | Based on symptom clusters; early warning |  |  | Fever with rash surveillance |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
Passive
Routine data from health facilities; least effort
OPD registers, death
certificates
Active
Active search by investigators; more accurate
House-to-house polio
surveys
Sentinel
Selected sentinel sites represent whole
population
Sentinel STI/HIV clinics
Syndromic
Based on symptom clusters; early warning
Fever with rash surveillance
B. Based on Coverage
Universal / Comprehensive: Entire population covered. Example: Civil Registration of Births &
Deaths
Sample / Sentinel: Representative sample. Example: National Family Health Survey (NFHS)
C. Based on Objectives
Epidemiological Surveillance: Monitor disease trends; detect outbreaks (e.g., Dengue
surveillance)
Laboratory Surveillance: Pathogen typing & drug resistance (e.g., AMR surveillance)
Nutritional Surveillance: Monitor nutritional status of vulnerable groups (e.g., SAM tracking)
Demographic Surveillance: Births, deaths, migration — DSS sites
Environmental Surveillance: Water, air quality monitoring; polio AFP surveillance
D. Special Types
Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP): India's national surveillance network — P
(peripheral), L (laboratory), S (specialist) forms
Event-Based Surveillance (EBS): Real-time monitoring of unusual health events, rumours via
media scanning
⚡ High Yield: WHO's 'Best Buys' for NCD control rely on STEPS surveillance data. 25×25 target =
25% reduction in premature NCD mortality by 2025.
★ Mnemonic: STEPS: Questionnaire → Physical → Biochemical (1→2→3)
## Q3. Surveillance of AEFI (Adverse Events Following Immunization)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (May 2025 — NEW)
Definition — AEFI
AEFI is any untoward medical occurrence which follows immunization and which does not necessarily
have a causal relationship with the usage of the vaccine. (WHO definition)
★ Mnemonic: "Any Medical Occurrence Post-Immunization" — does NOT imply causation
Classification of AEFI (WHO 2013 Brighton Collaboration)
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Category |  |  | Cause |  |  | Example |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Vaccine product-related |  |  | Inherent property of vaccine |  |  | OPV — VAPP |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Vaccine quality defect |  |  | Manufacturing defect |  |  |  | Aggregated particles → |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  | fever |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Immunization error |  |  | Wrong dose, site, technique |  |  | Abscess at injection site |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Immunization anxiety |  |  | Fear/anxiety about injection |  |  | Vasovagal syncope |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Coincidental |  |  | Unrelated — would have occurred anyway |  |  | Febrile seizures (URTI) |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
Vaccine product-related
Inherent property of vaccine
OPV — VAPP
Vaccine quality defect
Manufacturing defect
Aggregated particles →
fever
Immunization error
Wrong dose, site, technique
Abscess at injection site
Immunization anxiety
Fear/anxiety about injection
Vasovagal syncope

Unrelated — would have occurred anyway
Febrile seizures (URTI)
AEFI Surveillance System in India
Legal basis: Drugs & Cosmetics Act; National Pharmacovigilance Programme (PvPI)
Nodal body: Ministry of Health & Family Welfare; National AEFI Committee
Levels: Health worker → Block/PHC → District → State → National AEFI Committee
Reporting Criteria — Which AEFIs to Report?
ALL serious AEFIs must be reported within 24–48 hours:
Serious AEFI includes: Death, hospitalisation, life-threatening events, permanent disability,
anaphylaxis, encephalopathy, VAPP, seizures, sepsis at injection site, abscess
Non-serious AEFIs (fever, redness, pain) → record in health register; no immediate upward reporting
needed
Investigation of AEFI
1. Verify the event  →  2. Collect patient & vaccine details  →  3. Causality assessment  →  4. Report to
AEFI Committee  →  5. Corrective action
Causality Assessment: WHO causality categories: Consistent causal association / Inconsistent /
Indeterminate / Unclassifiable
Key Committees
District AEFI Committee: Chaired by CMO/CMHO; investigates all serious AEFIs
State AEFI Committee: Reviews district reports; provides technical guidance
National AEFI Committee: Apex body; issues national alerts; publishes annual report
⚡ High Yield: Anaphylaxis — most severe AEFI. Occurs within 30 min. Adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg IM
is treatment. Every vaccination point must have adrenaline.
★ Mnemonic: AEFI Classes: "VP-QE-IA-Anxiety-Coin" — Vaccine Product, Quality, Error,
Anxiety, Coincidental
## Q4. Herd Immunity and its Role in Infection Control  ⭐⭐⭐ (Nov 2023 — NEW)
Definition
Herd immunity (community immunity / population immunity) is the indirect protection afforded to
susceptible individuals when a sufficiently large proportion of a population has become immune to an
infection — either through vaccination or prior infection — thereby reducing the likelihood of infection
for individuals who lack immunity.
Mechanism
When immune individuals act as 'barriers', the chain of transmission is broken. Even non-immune
individuals are protected because the pathogen cannot easily find new susceptible hosts.
★ Mnemonic: "Sea of immune, island of susceptible — infection can't cross the sea"
Herd Immunity Threshold (HIT)
HIT = proportion of population that must be immune to halt epidemic spread.
Formula: HIT = 1 − (1/R₀)
Where R₀ = Basic Reproduction Number (average secondary cases from 1 primary case in fully
susceptible population)
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Disease |  |  | R₀ |  |  | HIT (approx.) |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Measles |  |  | 12–18 |  |  | 92–95% |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Polio |  |  | 5–7 |  |  | 80–85% |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | COVID-19 (original) |  |  | 2–3 |  |  | ~67% |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Smallpox |  |  | 5–7 |  |  | 83% |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Mumps |  |  | 4–7 |  |  | 75–86% |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
Measles
12–18
92–95%
Polio
5–7
80–85%
COVID-19 (original)
2–3
~67%
Smallpox
5–7
83%
Mumps
4–7
75–86%
Role in Infection Control
Protects vulnerable groups: Neonates, immunocompromised, elderly, pregnant women who
cannot be vaccinated
Outbreak prevention: High coverage prevents explosive outbreaks — seen with smallpox
eradication
Endemic control: Sustained high coverage maintains endemicity below epidemic threshold
Programme target: Vaccination programmes aim to exceed HIT to achieve herd immunity
Achieving Herd Immunity — Methods
Vaccination: PREFERRED — safe, ethical, no morbidity. Pulse Polio, measles campaigns
Natural infection: NOT recommended — leads to morbidity, mortality, and risk of more virulent
strains
Limitations
• Herd immunity does NOT apply to non-communicable diseases or non-person-to-person infections
(e.g., tetanus, rabies)
• Vaccine hesitancy creates susceptible pockets → outbreaks (measles resurgence in Europe 2018)
• Waning immunity may reduce herd effect over time
• Antigenic drift (influenza, COVID variants) may evade existing immunity
⚡ High Yield: Measles requires highest HIT (~95%) due to very high R₀. That is why 2 doses of
MMR are given in UIP.
★ Mnemonic: HIT = 1 − 1/R₀  ||  Higher R₀ → Higher HIT needed

subsections.title: 14. SCREENING — Applications, Criteria, Advantages & Disadvantages
subsections.content:
## Grade B ★★★★ | Asked 6x | NTRUHS SPM Paper I

Disadvantages
## Q1. Define Screening. Uses, Criteria and Validity of Screening Tests
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (2023)
Definition
Screening is the presumptive identification of unrecognized disease or defect by the application of
tests, examinations or other procedures that can be applied rapidly to sort out apparently healthy
individuals who probably have a disease from those who probably do not. (Commission on Chronic
Illness, USA, 1951)
KEY: Screening does NOT diagnose; it sorts into presumptively positive and presumptively negative
groups.
Types of Screening
Mass screening: Whole population screened regardless of risk (e.g., neonatal heel-prick for PKU)
Selective / High-risk: Target high-risk groups (e.g., smokers for lung cancer)
Multiphasic: Multiple tests in one visit (e.g., health camp — BP, CBG, BMI together)
Opportunistic: Screening during unrelated clinical visit (e.g., BP at dental clinic)
Case finding: Physician-initiated on individual patients
Uses of Screening
1. Early detection of disease at preclinical stage (→ better prognosis)
2. Reduction of disease burden at population level
3. Cost-effective intervention when treatment at early stage is cheaper
4. Epidemiological research — measure prevalence, natural history
5. Evaluation of prevention programmes

Sensitivity vs Specificity trade-off: Increasing sensitivity → decreasing specificity (and vice

Youden's Index: Sensitivity + Specificity − 1  (max = 1.0; best cut-off)

## Q2. Application of Screening for Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Oct 2025 — NEW)
Rationale for NCD Screening
NCDs have a long latent period, are usually asymptomatic early, and have effective interventions when
detected early — making them ideal candidates for screening programmes.

Special NCD Screening Programmes
Neonatal screening: Congenital hypothyroidism (TSH), PKU, sickle cell — heel-prick at 48–72 hrs
Antenatal screening: Gestational DM (GCT/OGTT), pre-eclampsia (BP, urine protein), anaemia
(Hb), HIV, syphilis
School health screening: Vision, hearing, dental, growth, scoliosis, skin
Occupational screening: Audiometry for noise-exposed workers, pulmonary function for miners
Effectiveness of NCD Screening
Evaluated by: ↓ in disease-specific mortality | ↓ stage at diagnosis | ↑ 5-year survival | Cost per
QALY/DALY averted
⚡ High Yield: VIA (Visual Inspection with Acetic Acid) is WHO-recommended for cervical cancer
screening in LMICs — cheap, no lab needed, same-visit treatment possible ('see & treat').
★ Mnemonic: NCD BCHOD: BP, Cancer (cervical, breast, oral), Hypo-thyroid, Obesity,
Diabetes

Key Biases in Screening — Must Know
Lead time bias: Survival appears longer because clock starts earlier (at screening), not because
patient lives longer
Length bias: Screening preferentially detects slow-growing tumours; aggressive tumours grow too
fast to be caught between screens
Volunteer bias (Selection bias): Screened population is healthier than non-screened — inflates
apparent benefit
Overdiagnosis bias: Detecting pseudodisease — abnormality that would never cause symptoms
or death
⚡ High Yield: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT) is the GOLD STANDARD to evaluate screening
programmes — eliminates lead time and length biases.
★ Mnemonic: Biases: "LLV-O" — Lead time, Length, Volunteer, Overdiagnosis
## Q4. Concept of Screening — Types, Criteria and Effectiveness  ⭐⭐⭐ (2018)
Concept of Screening
Screening is a secondary prevention strategy — does not prevent disease but detects it early to
improve outcomes. It is applied at the interface between primary prevention (preventing disease onset)
and tertiary prevention (limiting disability).
The concept rests on the idea that most chronic diseases have a preclinical detectable phase (PCDP)
during which intervention is more effective than after clinical disease onset.
Types of Screening (Detailed Classification)
By population covered: Mass → High-risk → Individual (case finding)
By setting: Community-based, hospital-based, workplace-based, school-based
By number of tests: Single test vs Multiphasic (battery of tests applied simultaneously)
By timing: Antenatal, neonatal, premarital, occupational, geriatric
By disease type: Communicable (TB — CBNAAT, sputum), NCD (HTN, DM, cancer), genetic
(sickle cell)
Criteria for Effectiveness of Screening
A screening programme is considered effective when:
↓ Specific mortality: Death rate from the target disease falls (RCT evidence required)
↓ Incidence of advanced disease: More cases detected at early stage
↑ Survival: True survival gain, not just lead time bias
Cost-effective: Cost per QALY/DALY averted within acceptable threshold
Acceptable false positive rate: <5–10% to avoid excessive follow-up burden
Evaluation Designs for Screening
RCT: Gold standard — randomize to screened vs unscreened; compare cause-specific mortality
Case-control study: Compare past screening history among cases vs controls
Cohort study: Follow screened vs unscreened — compare outcomes
Note: Survival rate is a POOR endpoint due to lead time and length biases — use disease-specific
MORTALITY RATE
Examples of Evaluated Screening Programmes
Cervical cancer (Pap/VIA): Proven to ↓ mortality — adopted in >100 countries
Breast cancer (mammography): ↓ mortality by ~15–20% in RCTs for women 50–69 yrs
Colorectal cancer (FOBT): ↓ mortality by ~15–33% in RCTs
Neonatal PKU: Dietary treatment from birth prevents intellectual disability
Prostate cancer (PSA): CONTROVERSIAL — high false positives, overdiagnosis; not
recommended for mass screening
⚡ High Yield: Wilson & Jungner 10 criteria are the standard for deciding whether to implement a
screening programme. Memorize all 10.
★ Mnemonic: Effectiveness = ↓ Mortality (not ↑ Survival) — True benefit shown only in RCT
## Q5. Criteria and Validity of Screening  ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
(Refer to Q1 for comprehensive answer — Wilson & Jungner 10 criteria and 2×2 validity table)

Sensitivity: a/(a+c)  — avoids false negatives — used for RULING OUT
Specificity: d/(b+d)  — avoids false positives — used for RULING IN
PPV: a/(a+b)  — rises with higher prevalence
NPV: d/(c+d)  — falls with higher prevalence
Reliability / Repeatability: Consistency of test results — measured by kappa statistic
⚡ High Yield: PPV and NPV depend on PREVALENCE; Sensitivity and Specificity are intrinsic to
the test (prevalence-independent).

memorization ★

subsections.title: 15. DEMOGRAPHIC CYCLE / TRANSITION / DIVIDEND / FERTILITY INDICATORS

## Grade B ★★★★ | Asked 6x | NTRUHS SPM Paper I

FERTILITY INDICATORS
## Q1. Discuss demographic cycle with suitable examples. ★★★★★ (2022, 2012)
Introduction
The Demographic Cycle (also called Demographic Transition Theory) describes the sequential change
in birth rates and death rates in a society as it progresses from a pre-industrial to an industrialized
economy. It was proposed by Warren Thompson (1929) and elaborated by Frank Notestein (1945).
Stages of the Demographic Cycle
The theory identifies FIVE stages (some sources describe 4), each characterized by distinct patterns of
birth rate (BR), death rate (DR), and natural growth rate (NGR = BR - DR):
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Stage |  |  | Birth Rate |  |  | Death Rate |  |  | Growth Rate |  |  | Example Countries |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | Stage I: High |  |  | Very High |  |  | Very High |  | Near Zero |  |  |  | Pre-historic societies; Few |  |
|  | Stationary |  |  | (40-50/1000) |  |  | (40-50/1000) |  |  |  |  |  | tribal populations today |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

|  | Expanding |  |  | (20-30/1000) |  |  | 15/1000) |  |  |  |  |  | Brazil |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |

Detailed Description of Each Stage
Stage I – High Stationary (Pre-Industrial): Both birth rate and death rate are high (40–50/1000).
Population remains stable/stagnant. High mortality due to famines, epidemics, and wars. No family
planning. Example: Pre-colonial India, Medieval Europe.
Stage II – Early Expanding (Industrial Revolution Phase): Death rate falls due to improved
sanitation, nutrition, and medical advances. Birth rate remains high. Population EXPLODES. This is
the most dangerous phase for resource management. Example: India in 1920s–1970s.
Stage III – Late Expanding (Transitional): Birth rate begins to fall due to urbanization, education
of women, use of contraception, rise in cost of living. Death rate is already low and stable. Growth
decelerates. Example: India currently (BR ~20, DR ~7/1000).
Stage IV – Low Stationary (Post-Industrial): Both birth and death rates are low. Zero population
growth. Aging population. Example: Western Europe, North America.
Stage V – Declining: Birth rate falls BELOW death rate → negative natural growth rate →
population shrinks. Concerns: labour shortage, pension burden. Example: Germany (BR 9, DR
12/1000), Japan.
India's Position in Demographic Cycle
India is currently in STAGE III (Late Expanding) of the Demographic Transition:
•
Birth Rate: ~20/1000 (2020 SRS data)
•
Death Rate: ~6.2/1000
•
Natural Growth Rate: ~13.8/1000
•
TFR: 2.0 (National Family Health Survey-5, 2019-21) — near replacement level
•
15 states have already achieved TFR ≤ 2.1 (replacement level)
Demographic Gap
The gap between the falling death rate and the (slower-falling) birth rate is called the 'Demographic Gap.'
This gap = period of maximum population growth. India's demographic gap was widest in 1960s–1980s.
★ The Demographic Transition Theory explains population dynamics through 5 stages. India
is in Stage III with a TFR of 2.0, approaching replacement level. Key determinants: education,
urbanization, female literacy, family planning programs.
✔ Exam Tip: Draw the classic S-curve/J-curve diagram showing BR and DR trends across stages.
Examiners reward visual representation.
## Q2. What are the different processes of demography? Discuss fertility indicators.
★★★★ (2023)
Processes of Demography
Demography is the scientific study of human populations. The three fundamental demographic processes
are:
•
1. Fertility: The reproductive performance of a population — refers to actual live births occurring in
a population.
•
2. Mortality: Deaths occurring in a population. Measured by death rate, IMR, MMR, etc.
•
3. Migration: Movement of people into (immigration) or out of (emigration) a geographic area.
Internal migration (rural-to-urban) is a major driver of demographic change in India.
These three processes determine:
•
Population size
•
Population composition (age, sex distribution)
•
Population distribution (urban vs rural)
Fertility Indicators — Detailed Classification
A. Crude Birth Rate (CBR)
Definition: Number of live births per 1000 mid-year population in a given year.
Formula: CBR = (Number of live births / Mid-year total population) × 1000
Normal range: High: >40; Moderate: 20–40; Low: <20
India (2020): ~20/1000
Limitation: Includes entire population in denominator (including non-women of reproductive age)
— hence crude.
B. General Fertility Rate (GFR)
Formula: GFR = (Live births / Women aged 15–49 years) × 1000
More refined than CBR as denominator is restricted to women of reproductive age (WRA).
C. Age-Specific Fertility Rate (ASFR)
Formula: ASFR = (Live births to women in age group X / Mid-year women in age group X) × 1000
Calculated for each 5-year age group: 15–19, 20–24, 25–29, 30–34, 35–39, 40–44, 45–49.
Use: Identifies peak fertile age; basis for calculating TFR
D. Total Fertility Rate (TFR) — MOST IMPORTANT
Definition: Average number of children a woman would have if she lived through her entire reproductive
period (15–49 years) at currently observed ASFR.
Formula: TFR = 5 × Σ(ASFR for all 7 age groups) — multiplied by 5 as each age group spans 5 years.
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | TFR Value |  |  | Interpretation |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | <2.1 |  |  | Declining population (long-term) |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |  |  |
|  | India TFR (NFHS-5, 2019-21) |  |  | 2.0 — Below replacement level for the first time! |  |
